title: GPG stalls on database_open lock when using pass driver for secrets
body:
When trying to use secrets with the passdriver it fails with an opaque error code "exit status 2".

This happens both when creating the secret, and (assuming you manually create the secret) when you try to load the secret.

The crux of this issue seems to stem from this message

> gpg: Note: database_open 134217901 waiting for lock (held by 6970) ...

Where PID 6970 is keyboxd

> admin       6970  0.0  0.0 231492  4276 ?        Ss   12:18   0:00 keyboxd --homedir /home/admin/.gnupg --daemon
> admin       7041  0.0  0.0 305224  2376 ?        Ss   12:18   0:00 keyboxd --homedir /home/admin/.gnupg --daemon

## Repro

I am running Fedora 42  (Server Edition), on a PowerEdge 2950. It has 2 2-core CPUs, 32 GiB of RAM, and these issues are occurring with files on the boot XFS SSD. Podman is being run rootlessly, but these errors appear when running the command with sudo.

You of course will need a GPG key installed.

It's worth noting here that if you haven't trusted the key you get the same "exit status 2" error, but it happens immediately, and I haven't bothered diagnosing what error messages it produces in the background.

Once the key is trusted you can attempt to create the secret. In this case I used this test command

> printf password123 | podman secret create test_secret --driver=pass --driver-opts=key=ABCD1234 -

This will then stall and eventually report

> Error: creating secret test_secret: exit status 2

If we run `ps auxf` while it is stalled we should see something to the effect of

```
admin      46016  1.4  0.1 1942312 44588 pts/0   Sl+  16:07   0:00      |   \\_ podman secret create test_secret --driver=pass --driver-opts=key=ABCD1234 -
admin      46028  0.0  0.0  11704  4500 pts/0    SL+  16:07   0:00      |       \\_ gpg --encrypt -r ABCD1234 -o /home/admin/02595e1876d7790afb3eedb42.gpg
```

This shows that the command that is stalling is the GPG command. Running this command manually works without a problem.

This repo discards StdErr, but we can still read it with the command

> ps auxf | grep -E 'gpg +--' | awk '{print $2}' | xargs -I{} cat /proc/{}/fd/2

This command has 4 parts: `ps auxf` prints out the list of processes; `grep -E 'gpg +--'` finds the GPG process, we need some special RegEx here to exclude the `grep` process that would otherwise also be returned; `awk '{print $2}'` came from ChatGPT and extracts the PID; `xargs -I{} /proc/{}/fd/2` also came from ChatGPT and passes the PID into the path for `ls` and loads the File Descriptor for StdErr.

This will produce at least one log message along the lines of

> gpg: Note: database_open 134217901 waiting for lock (held by 6970) ...

The PID 6970 that is holding the lock belongs to one of the instances of `keyboxd`

> admin       6970  0.0  0.0 231492  4276 ?        Ss   12:18   0:00 keyboxd --homedir /home/admin/.gnupg --daemon
> admin       7041  0.0  0.0 305224  2376 ?        Ss   12:18   0:00 keyboxd --homedir /home/admin/.gnupg --daemon

## Code Sources

The vague error appears to come from [secrets.go#L237](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/secrets.go#L237)

```go
err = driver.Store(secr.ID, data)
if err != nil {
	return "", fmt.Errorf("creating secret %s: %w", name, err)
}
```

There is another [location](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/secrets.go#L242) that could also be the source, but it is unlikely since the default driver works.

This then traces to [passdriver.go#L139](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L139) 

```go
func (d *Driver) Store(id string, data []byte) error {
	if _, err := d.Lookup(id); err == nil {
		return fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", id, define.ErrSecretIDExists)
	}
	in := bytes.NewReader(data)
	key, err := d.getPath(id)
	if err != nil {
		return err
	}
	return d.gpg(context.TODO(), in, nil, "--encrypt", "-r", d.KeyID, "-o", key)
}
```

which in turn executes the GPG command at [passdriver.go#166](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L166).

```go
func (d *Driver) gpg(ctx context.Context, in io.Reader, out io.Writer, args ...string) error {
	if d.GPGHomedir != "" {
		args = append([]string{"--homedir", d.GPGHomedir}, args...)
	}
	cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "gpg", args...)
	cmd.Env = os.Environ()
	cmd.Stdin = in
	cmd.Stdout = out
	cmd.Stderr = io.Discard
	return cmd.Run()
}
```

It seems important to note that shortly before the GPG encrypt command is called that [Lookup](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L114) is called

```go
func (d *Driver) Lookup(id string) ([]byte, error) {
	out := &bytes.Buffer{}
	key, err := d.getPath(id)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, err
	}
	if err := d.gpg(context.TODO(), nil, out, "--decrypt", key); err != nil {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", id, define.ErrNoSuchSecret)
	}
	if out.Len() == 0 {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", id, define.ErrNoSuchSecret)
	}
	return out.Bytes(), nil
}
```

## Additional Testing

I did clone this repo and do some manual testing, but it was limited and had to be done with unit tests.

I essentially recreated the exact command I would be using, but made sure that [Stdout & Stderr](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L164-L165) were redirected. Additionally I logged the GPG commands before than ran. This results in the output

>Running command: gpg --decrypt /home/admin/test_secret.gpg
>gpg: can't open '/home/admin/test_secret.gpg': No such file or directory
>gpg: decrypt_message failed: No such file or directory
>Running command: gpg --encrypt -r ABCD1234 -o /home/admin/test_secret.gpg

Based on what I can find, this indicates to me that the decrypt GPG command could be locking the database and it is not getting unlocked by the time the encrypt command runs. I'm not entirely sure why this works in the unit test but not with Podman, if I had to guess, I'd wager that running `go test` rebuilds the code without optimizations, and so there is enough of a delay that it just works. A pitfall with this hypothesis is that the "waiting for lock" message can appear multiple times, indicating that it is at least trying several times.

I also manually tried tossing the secret into `secrets.json`, and running a container with

> podman run --secret=test_secret debian cat "/run/secrets/test_secret"

Which likewise stalls and errors out with

> Error: 02595e1876d7790afb3eedb42: no such secret

But intercepting StdErr shows that it is actually the same problem as when creating secrets.

## Final Notes

I'm not the only one to have this issue. My friend is running a similar setup, albeit with very different server specs, and was encountering this issue, which led me here.

I've tried running the GPG commands manually many times and don't encounter this error. It only happens when they run as a result of the `podman` command.

It would also be nice if [Stdout & Stderr](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L164-L165) weren't simply discarded for the GPG command. I would prefer that StdErr is always written to the console, but having it only write with a verbose flag is still better than the debugging I had to do. I'd even accept an obscure `--driver-opt`.

comments.body:
This issue has been quite the adventure, and I've been chasing it for almost two years now. It first appeared in Fedora 39 and has remained present through Fedora 43. This coincides with the switch to keyboxd in Fedora 39.

## Testing

I first noticed this in the same case as above, where creating a podman secrete would fail as follows:

```bash
podman secret create --driver=pass --driver-opts key=$GPGKEY $NAME $FILE

Error: creating secret $NAME: exit status 2
gpg: Note: database_open 134217901 waiting for lock (held by $PID) ...
gpg: keydb_search_first failed: Connection timed out
```

After some digging around in the gpg docs, I came across the `gpgconf --unlock pubring.db` command that unlocks the public key database. After which Podman can create secretes. However, when attempting to use GPG, it now fails in the same way Podman did before:

```bash
gpg -v --debug-all --list-keys

gpg: Note: database_open 134217901 waiting for lock (held by $PID) ...
gpg: keydb_search_first failed: Connection timed out
```

Similarly, the first one run after reboot would succeed, but the other process would get stuck waiting for the lock.

I saw numerous online recommendations to disable `use-keyboxd`. However, this doesn't seem like a great long-term solution.

I decided to run a trace on podman, as it is the only program that seems to cause this lockup with gpg.

```bash
strace -DfyY -e 'trace=connect,execve,newfstatat,read,write,' -o log.txt podman secret create --driver=pass --driver-opts key=$GPGKEY $NAME $FILE

<strace> execve("/usr/sbin/podman", ["podman", "secret", "create", "--driver=pass", "--driver-opts", "key= $GPGKEY"..., "$NAME", "$FILE"], 0x7ffd371d6880 /* 23 vars */) = 0
<podman> execve("/usr/sbin/gpg", ["gpg", "--encrypt", "-r", "$GPGKEY"..., "-o", "/home/username/8fd7fe036d9d58a54"...], 0xc0005380f0 /* 28 vars */) = 0
<gpg> read(3</home/username/.gnupg/common.conf>, "use-keyboxd\n", 8192) = 12
<gpg> read(3</home/username/.gnupg/common.conf>, "", 8192) = 0
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/run/user/0", 0x7ffea94167e0, 0) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/var/run/user/0", 0x7ffea94167e0, 0) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/home/username/.gnupg/S.keyboxd", {st_mode=S_IFSOCK|0700, st_size=0, ...}, 0) = 0
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/home/username/.gnupg/S.keyboxd", {st_mode=S_IFSOCK|0700, st_size=0, ...}, 0) = 0
<gpg> connect(3<socket:[17996]>, {sa_family=AF_UNIX, sun_path="/home/username/.gnupg/S.keyboxd"}, 33) = 0
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: keydb_search failed: Connec"..., 46) = 46
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: $GPGKEY"..., 76) = 76
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: [stdin]: encryption failed:"..., 53) = 53
```

It appears that when podman is running GPG, its `$UID` is incorrectly set to `0`. However, `$USER` seems to be set correctly to `username`. Checking GPG's configuration, we can indeed confirm that it should create the socket at `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` for user 1000: `/run/user/1000/gnupg`, not root: `/run/user/0/gnupg`.

```bash
gpgconf -L

homedir:/home/username/.gnupg
socketdir:/run/user/1000/gnupg
agent-socket:/run/user/1000/gnupg/S.gpg-agent
keyboxd-socket:/run/user/1000/gnupg/S.keyboxd
```

Looking through GNUPG's source, I was able to locate the [_gnupg_socketdir_internal homedir.c:1149](https://github.com/gpg/gnupg/blob/master/common/homedir.c#L1149) function, which is responsible for creating the sockets, skipping ahead to [homedir.c:1272](https://github.com/gpg/gnupg/blob/master/common/homedir.c#L1272), we can see GPG has a list of paths it will attempt to make the sockets at. If the path does not exist or is not owned by the current user, the operation will fail and try the next one. In the case above, GPG will fall through all of them until it falls back to `$HOME/.gnupg`. Since standard GPG places its socket at `/run/user/1000/gnupg`, this explains why they both attempt to start their own instance. This results in the second instance failing because the first instance has already locked the keyrings.

## Potential Fix

I would assume the fix would be to add `SysProcAttr.Credential` to the command code referenced above by @donovan-maas-gs at [passdriver.go:166](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L166). It would also be incredibly helpful to modify `cmd.Stderr = io.Discard` to allow it to print out the `stderr` with either a driver-opts or verbose flag as mentioned above. However, I am unsure of the implications of this fix and would greatly appreciate input from the maintainers before opening a pr to resolve this.

## Workaround

While investigating this issue, I encountered several similar cases involving secrets and image signing, specifically using hardware GPG keys: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13539 and https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23659. I also came across an issue that proposes allowing the configuration of the GPG program used by podman via a flag or `containers.conf`, which could also be beneficial in resolving these issues: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/15838.

While the workarounds from some of these other threads are functional, they are not ideal because they are quite manual and introduce other issues on multi-user systems. Such as symlinking or using Assuan's socket redirects from `/run/user/0/gnupg` and `$HOME/.gnupg` to `/run/user/1000/gnupg`.

However, I was able to find a very poorly documented GPG config file [/usr/bin/gpgconf.ctl](https://www.gnupg.org/documentation/manuals/gnupg/Files-used-by-gpgconf.html). By setting `socketdir = $HOME/.gnupg` you can force GPG's default socket directory to its fallback path, which resolves podman's `$UID` issue. As long as all your programs are running `gpg-agent` and not attempting to connect to a hardcoded socket path directly, this should not be an issue. As far as I can tell, `gpg-agent` discourages directly connecting to a hardcoded socket path anyway due to its potential to cause race conditions.

```ini
rootdir = /usr/
sysconfdir = /etc/gnupg
socketdir = $HOME/.gnupg
```

On the secrets side, I believe the secret is extracted to a plain file during [container creation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/5134dd3bee8312f08147190a7cbea49f196038cb/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L473), before starting the container, so it might be possible? Though my memory is shaky on that area to be honest.

> On the secrets side, I believe the secret is extracted to a plain file during [container creation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/5134dd3bee8312f08147190a7cbea49f196038cb/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L473), before starting the container, so it might be possible? Though my memory is shaky on that area to be honest.

From what I recall from my testing, that is accurate. The file is decrypted and stored plain text in the `/run` directory, I believe it comes from a mount for a subfolder of the `~/.local` directory with some very strict permissions.

## Testing

I decided to investigate running gpg with a specific UID using the `SysProcAttr.Credential` attribute of go exec in [gpg passdriver.go:157](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/blob/main/common/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L157) function. During this, I noticed that the following env variables were already being passed through to gpg: `_CONTAINERS_USERNS_CONFIGURED`, `_CONTAINERS_ROOTLESS_UID`, `_CONTAINERS_ROOTLESS_GID`, and were correctly configured. This appears to indicate that something upstream is incorrectly setting the namespace, and I was unsure if setting the UID for the gpg command alone is the correct solution.

After running some more traces with the following types: `%creds,%desc,%file,%fstat,%memory,%network,%process`, I was able to narrow it down to the following:

```bash
strace -DfyY -e 'trace=access,connect,execve,geteuid,getuid,getegid,getgid,setresgid,setresuid,setns,newfstatat,openat,read,write' -o log.txt podman secret create --driver=pass --driver-opts key=$GPGKEY $NAME $FILE

<strace> execve("/usr/sbin/podman", ["podman", "secret", "create", "--driver=pass", "--driver-opts", "key=$GPGKEY"..., "$NAME", "$FILE"], 0x7ffd98f79ef0 /* 23 vars */) = 0
<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/self/fd", O_RDONLY|O_NONBLOCK|O_CLOEXEC|O_DIRECTORY) = 3</proc/9098/fd>
<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/self/cmdline", O_RDONLY) = 4</proc/9098/cmdline>
<podman> read(4</proc/9098/cmdline>, "podman\0secret\0create\0--driver=pa"..., 512) = 113
<podman> read(4</proc/9098/cmdline>, "", 399) = 0
<podman> geteuid()                      = 1000
<podman> access("/etc/containers/podman_preexec_hooks.txt", F_OK) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
<podman> geteuid()                      = 1000
<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/pause.pid", O_RDONLY) = 4</run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/pause.pid>
<podman> read(4</run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/pause.pid>, "1672", 11) = 4
<podman> geteuid()                      = 1000
<podman> getegid()                      = 1000
<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/1672/ns/user", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 5<user:[4026532286]>
<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/1672/ns/mnt", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 6<mnt:[4026532287]>
<podman> setns(5<user:[4026532286]>, 0) = 0
<podman> setns(6<mnt:[4026532287]>, 0)  = 0
<podman> setresgid(0, 0, 0)             = 0
<podman> setresuid(0, 0, 0)             = 0
<podman> getuid()                       = 0
<podman> geteuid()                      = 0
<podman> getgid()                       = 0
<podman> execve("/usr/sbin/gpg", ["gpg", "--encrypt", "-r", "$GPGKEY"..., "-o", "/home/username/ebb21ccef54e705c0"...], 0xc00050e000 /* 28 vars */) = 0
<gpg> getuid()                          = 0
<gpg> read(3</home/username/.gnupg/common.conf>, "use-keyboxd\n", 8192) = 12
<gpg> read(3</home/username/.gnupg/common.conf>, "", 8192) = 0
<gpg> getuid()                          = 0
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/run/user/0", 0x7ffc01bc2c00, 0) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
<gpg> getuid()                          = 0
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/var/run/user/0", 0x7ffc01bc2c00, 0) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/home/username/.gnupg/S.keyboxd", {st_mode=S_IFSOCK|0700, st_size=0, ...}, 0) = 0
<gpg> newfstatat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/home/username/.gnupg/S.keyboxd", {st_mode=S_IFSOCK|0700, st_size=0, ...}, 0) = 0
<gpg> connect(3<socket:[]>, {sa_family=AF_UNIX, sun_path="/home/username/.gnupg/S.keyboxd"}, 33) = 0
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: Note: database_open 1342179"..., 70) = 70
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: keydb_search failed: Connec"..., 46) = 46
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: $GPGKEY"..., 76) = 76
<gpg> write(2<pipe:[]>, "gpg: [stdin]: encryption failed:"..., 53) = 53
<gpg> +++ exited with 2 +++
<podman> --- SIGCHLD {si_signo=SIGCHLD, si_code=CLD_EXITED, si_pid=<gpg>, si_uid=0, si_status=2, si_utime=0, si_stime=2 /* 0.02 s */} ---
```

From this, it is clear that at some point, podman is setting the namespaces' real and effective user/group IDs to root. I was able to match the following trace lines to [podman/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c:init:430](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L430) code. I have included the matching lines below for reference:

* [`<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/self/fd", O_RDONLY|O_NONBLOCK|O_CLOEXEC|O_DIRECTORY) = 3</proc/9098/fd>`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L450)
* [`<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/self/cmdline", O_RDONLY) = 4</proc/9098/cmdline>`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L490)
* [`<podman> geteuid() = 1000`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L499)
* [`<podman> access("/etc/containers/podman_preexec_hooks.txt", F_OK) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L500)
* [`<podman> geteuid() = 1000`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L525)
* [`<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/pause.pid", O_RDONLY) = 4</run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/pause.pid>`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L557)
* [`<podman> read(4</run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/pause.pid>, "1672", 11) = 4`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L561)
* [`<podman> geteuid() = 1000`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L571)
* [`<podman> getegid() = 1000`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L572)
* [`<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/1672/ns/user", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 5<user:[4026532286]>`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L574)
* [`<podman> openat(AT_FDCWD</home/username>, "/proc/1672/ns/mnt", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 6<mnt:[4026532287]>`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L578)
* [`<podman> setns(5<user:[4026532286]>, 0) = 0`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L582)
* [`<podman> setns(6<mnt:[4026532287]>, 0)  = 0`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L588)
* [`<podman> getgid() = 0`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L601)
* [`<podman> setresuid(0, 0, 0) = 0`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.c#L607)

After inspecting the Go wrapper [rootless_linux.go:24](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/rootless/rootless_linux.go#L24) that appears to call the C code above, I noticed it includes [container-libs/storage/pkg/unshare/unshare.c](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/blob/main/storage/pkg/unshare/unshare.c). This reminded me of the [podman unshare](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-unshare.1.html) command, which clarified what's going on here. The gpg command is being run in Podman's default namespace, so running as root and mapped to the user is the intended behavior here.

```bash
podman unshare cat /proc/self/uid_map /proc/self/gid_map

         0       1000          1
         1     524288      65536
         0       1000          1
         1     524288      65536
```

## Potential Fixes

While contemplating some possible fixes, such as:

* Some namespace magic:
	* Mapping both 0 and UID in the namespace to the user's UID. This would allow go exec to run gpg with `SysProcAttr.Credential` set to the UID.
	* Create a sub-namespace with 0 mapped to UID. I had done some testing with the `unshare --map-user $UID` command that seemed promising.

* A paragraph in the solution @mtrmac suggested in [containers/podman#23659](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23659) caught my eye.
	> … in practice, here, the GPG subprocesses are executed by a C library libgpgme, so we just don’t have that kind of control; we would have to introduce an extra IPC layer from podman+c/image to our own single-use GPG server running in a modified namespace, which then further uses libgpgme to run GPG subprocesses.
	
We do not need to implement any of that complicated IPC stuff; that is what sockets are for. Sure enough, a search turned up another poorly documented GPG command [gpg-connect-agent](https://www.gnupg.org/documentation/manuals/gnupg/gpg_002dconnect_002dagent.html) which has a `-S` arg.
> Connect to socket name assuming this is an Assuan style server. Do not run any special initializations or environment checks. This may be used to directly connect to any Assuan style socket server.

Funnily enough, it also has `--chuid uid`.
> Change the current user to UID, which may either be a number or a name. This can be used from the root account to run gpg-connect-agent for another user. If uid is not the current UID, a standard PATH is set and the envvar GNUPGHOME is unset. To override the latter, the option --homedir can be used. This option has only an effect when used on the command line. This option currently has no effect at all on Windows.

## Proposed Fix

* To Fix [containers/podman#13539](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13539) & [containers/podman#15838](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/15838)
	* gpg function in [passdriver.go:157](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/blob/main/common/pkg/secrets/passdriver/passdriver.go#L157)
		* Replace `gpg` with `gpg-connect-agent`.
		* Adding default socket search paths: `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/gnupg/S.gpg-agent` & `$HOME/.gnupg/S.gpg-agent`.
			* Maybe these should be a config option in `containers.conf`?
	* init function in [create.go:36](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/cmd/podman/secrets/create.go#L36)
		* Add a driver option for passing in a GPG socket.
		* Add a driver option for printing `stderr`.
	* [Podman Secret Create Docs](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/source/markdown/podman-secret-create.1.md)
		* Update Documentation.

* However, I am unsure how to resolve [containers/podman#23659](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23659)
	* Browsing through [container-libs/image/signature](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/tree/main/image/signature) it appears image signing uses [GPGME](https://www.gnupg.org/documentation/manuals/gpgme) instead of calling gpg directly. I don't know at this time if there is a similar solution to fix this as well. @mtrmac, any further input or context you have on that code would be greatly appreciated.

Any feedback or implications of making these changes would also be greatly appreciated.
